o Independent of last Thursday we read :—" Our
Tr::ile:I::orrespg]ndent wires—Mr. Redmond Roche, J.P.,
Maglass, has beon appointed sub-sheriff of Kerry, in
succession to the late Mr. Goodman. Mr. Rocho is the
first Catholic who has occupied tho position for 50 years,
and his appointment reflects credit on the 1§beml spirib
of tho High Sheriff, Mr. Latchford, who is a Protes-
tant, and who mado the appointment. "I’."here wero 50
a.pplica,nts for the position. Mr. Roche’s solection is

very popular.”’ :
Well, surely there’s the tame Catholic with a vengo-

this Cabholic County of Kerry there has ngbv
ﬁgzﬁ'ul&ohoﬁc Sub-Sheriff for 60 years, and whon, in

tho year of grace 1906, a Papist is elected, the fact,
according to tho Zndependent’s Tralee correspondent,
“reflects credit on the liberal spirit of the High Sheriff !’
Wo are told there were fifty applicants, and we hope
that Mr. Redmond Roche got the appointment not be-
causo ho was an ““ Idolator,”” but because he was judged
to bo the best man for it.

The paragraph we have quoted shows how deeply tho
half-slave spirit engendered by generations of Protes-
tant Ascendancy, is embedded in the nature of many
present-day Irish “Idolators.” We suppose if this
Independent correspondent was accustomed to get a
kick once a week during fifty years from some Ascend-
ancy dog-boy, he would give the dog-boy a testimonial
as’a token of his appreciation of his liberal spirit,”” if
he ceased the weekly kick after fifty years. And who,
we wonder, is Mr. Latchford, a Protestant, whose
“liberal spirit’’ is so emphasised by the fact that a
mere ““ Idolator ’’ was made Sub-Sheriff of Kerry after
an uninterrupted period of fifty years of “Saved ’’ occu-
pation? Wo presume he is in business, and if he is, no
doubt, his customers must necessarily be mainly Kerry
“TIdolators.”” And because this gentleman elects a
Kerry “Idolator ” to the post of Sub-Sheriff in one of
the most “Idolatrous’’ counties in Ireland,- this Trales
correspondent of the Zndependent cannot refrain from
paying a tribute to his “ liberal spirit!"’

The Irish T'imes is anxious to know who are the Irish.
In the course of an editorial article it asks—“In the
first place, who are ¢ the Irish?” Does the phrase include
the inhabitants of Amtrim and Armagh, to say nothing
of the thousands of loyal Unionists in the other three
provinces, or is it confined strictly to those who profess
Nationalist principles?”’ Poor old Irish Times! We
suppose it would like to make out that its imported
Editor is Irish!

The sub-agent at Longford of the Bank of Ireland
has been promoted to the head office in the “ Old House
in College Green.””  One might expect that a collec-.
tion of old toadies in Longford would be fluttering about
collecting reluctant half-sovereigns from ‘poor people,
for the purpose of presenting a testimonial to this bank
clerk, who would no doubt entertain the promoters hos-
pitably out of part of the “hat’’ at one of the local
licensed establishments.  The paper of a great Nation-
alist M.P. we see is early in the field suggesting a testi-
monial. The Longford Leader, which is run by Mr.
J. P. Farrell, M.P., says: —* He (the bank clerk) leaves
town with the heartiest goodwill of all sections of the
Bank’s customers, who will, we hope, seize the oppor-
tunity of his departure by making him the recipient of
a presentation which will convey, in more tangible and
lasting form than can mere words, their appreciation of
his many good qualities during his residence amongst
us.””  Well wo hope otherwise ; we hope that the people
of Longford will allow this bank clerk to go to his pro-
moted desk in “ the Old House in College Green *’ quietly
and without any purse of sovereigns and champagne
“drawbacks.” It would be fitter for Mr. Farrell to be
fighting for free and open competitive examinations for
the clerkships of this rather exclusive Bank of Ireland,
so-called, than advocating a tax on some of the unfortu-
nate people of Longford to make up a “hat’ for a
Bank of Ireland Clerk who has got promotion.

Bverybody’s Monthly is the title of a new paper pub-
lished in Ireland, or rather in Belfast, which
is mot a distinction without a difference, _a-ud

THE LEADER.

which styles itself ¢ Organ of the Irish Tem-

- perance Movement.’”” Communications of a busi-
ness nature are to be addressed to the Secretary, Mr.
Wm. Wilkinson, at the offices of the Irish Temperance
League, Belfast, so we assume that Zverybody’s Monthly
is the official organ of the Irish Temperance League of
Belfast, which we understand got, or is to get, half the
proceeds of the All Ireland Temperance Bazaar. We
have not yet seen the accounts of that Bazaar, though
they were promised by Mr. Wilkinson some months ago.
It will be remembered that Mr. Moriarty, the saved
ex-Secretary of the Irish Association for the Prevention
of Intemperance, which Association also was to get half
the All Ireland Bazaar profits, became a very decided
bankrupt.  As individuals who spent money at that
“all creeds—all classes ”’ bazaar we would like to see a
clear statement of accounts, as promised, as carly as
possible.

It may be remembered that the *“ Irish *’ Temperance
League of Belfast published an official organ, which con-
tained a signed article in one of its issues that had some
insulting and scurrilous references to the Catholic faith.
We drew attention to this lapse from the “ non-sec-
tarian ’ platform, and whether as a result of these
references the old official organ has been dropped, and
EBverybody’s Monthly established, we can only surmise.
Tt is significant anyway that the new organ of the Irish
Temperance League of Belfast is put forward as one
that will “ hurt no conscience.”” Evidently it is truly
going to be a marvel amongst publications! If we were
running a temperance magazine we would announce that
in every issue we would put nettles on Mr. Bung’s con-

- science!

A glance through the first number, which has been
sent us for notice, convinces us that it is a British, and
at least a decidedly non-Trish, paper. We have not
discovered a word of Irish in the first number of this
organ of the “ Irish’’ Temperance movement! Under
the general heading, “ Master Builders,”’ we see, though
we have not read, notices of ‘“ Geofry Chaucer, 1340-
1400, Founder of English Literature,” and “ BEarl
Chatham, 1708-1778, Founder of British Democracy.”
Nothing very Irish about these items. Perhaps next
week we may find—* William Wilkinson, Founder of
Irish National Temperance !

In a page of “Pearls from the Poets’’ we do not see
any quotation from an Irish poet, not even one from
an Anglo-Irish versifier. In the next—the October—
issue of Bverybody’s Monthly we hope to see an article
in Irish; and by that time also, we would like to have
before us a statement of the accounts of the All Ireland
Bazaar.

The anti-Irish in Ireland are badly off for something
with which to attempt to inflame the anti-Irish spirit.
The Irish Times, of the imported editor, made a
desperate effort last Friday to incite the anti-Irish pas-
sion, and the effort is significant and conveys its salutary
lesson. The white gloves that judges receive in Ireland
are rod rags to the enemies of the Irish nation like
Arnott’s Irish Times, of the imported editor. Nothing
would suit Dublin Castle and the Zrish Times better
than to be able to cry crocodile tears over Irish “ law-
lessness and crime.”” ~ It would suit their game admir-
ablyin the attempt to dwarf, if they cannot stop, legisla-
tion favourable to the sound growth of this historic
country.

The recent case where a young man was sentenced to
prison—probably there will be an appeal—for an assault,
on a Tommy Atkins in Donegal is seized upon by the
Irish Times with avidity as a stick with which to beat

the anti-Irish drum. It is plain that anything like
assaults on Tommy Atkins individually—which are at
best only brawls—are bread and butter to the re-
actionary and anti-Irish forces ropresented by the
Irish Times. Nothing would give greater satisfaction

Dublin Castle than an attack once a weck on a fow
Tommy Atkins. It would supply a grand case for con-

SEPTEMBER 1, 1908.

sumption by the Big Brother, particularly when cooked
as the Castle gang can cook these things. We would
not be surprised if a few spies were on the prowl these
days oendoavouring t6 induce unsuspecting and well-
meaning youths to so act as to supply muqh wanted
material that the anti-Irish gang cquld use, with a view
to working up the passions, prejudices and fears of the
Big Brother.

Fortunately the Zrish Times has been in a great hurry
to make capital out of the Donegal l?rawl; it has shown
its hand and has supplied a warning to the country
as to the uses which incidents of this kind will be put to.
The Irish Times says:—Tho Cabinet which is pre-
paring a large measure for the extension of Irish self-
government cannot afford to overlook the sort of Irish
ideas ’ that find favour in Donegal and Wexford.”

Probably whatever reforms are to be proposed for Ire-
land next year will be small things at the best, but
small as they may be the Zrish Times would be delighted
if it could make them smaller by playing to its gallery
and to the Big Brother if only it could lay hands on a
few things like the Donegal brawl as instruments for
playing its tunes.

Mr. Samuel Young, M.P.,is on the track of the tract
romancers. A romance entitled “ The Adventures of a
Bible,” by Rev. J. H. Townsend, D.D., Vicar of St.
Mark’s, Tunbridge Wells, fell into his hands. This
high-flying romance gives an account of a Mrs. Blake,
of Dublin, who went to a ‘ Mission Hall ’ by mistake,
when she was looking for a place of amusement. She
got a Bible there, and the romance states that she had
never scen one before. This Bible, according to Rev.
Dr. Townsend’s romance, “ converted ** her. A priest,
the romance relates, took the Bible from her in anger.
Mus. Blake, of the romance, called on the priest for her
Bible. She was met by a nun who refused her admis-
sion, but conveyed a cwrse from the young priest, who
had been ill. Mrs. Blake, of the romance, called again
at the priest’s house and was tcld by the nun that she,
the nun, had told a lie the last time, that the priest
had since died, that before his death he, by reading the
Bible, had found forgiveness, and blessed her for the
book, and that the nun had left the convent and found
peace by reading the same Bible. This is a synopsis of
the romance that fell into Mr. Young’s hands.

On July 29th last Mr. Young wrote to the author of
this “story,”” the Rev. J. H. Townsend, D.D., Vicar
of St. Mark’s, Tunbridge Wells. He asked the author
where Mrs. Blake, of Dublin, lived, the name of the
young priest, tho name of the convent and of the nun.
The Rev. J. H. Townsend was not in a position
to supply the information asked. Of course not. And
tho excuse?  Of cowrse “to save some from relentless
persecution I am obliged to keep in strict secrecy the
particulars which have been confided to me.’’

Mr. Young very politely replied to this Rev. author
that “T fear the plea of secrecy will hardly cover the
incredibility of your conversion story.”’ Well, hardly.
As to the dread of persecution Mr. Young remarks:—
“It is difficult to conceive of any danger to a convert
in Treland. All such are cagerly taken up and pro-
vided for.  Notably, two or three well-known converts
to Protestantism have and are making a good thing of
it.  Could thero be any danger to your converts if I give
A guaranteo in tho shape of a deposit in money, which
amount you can wmention, to secure safety, or you can
soud particulars in a letter marked confidential, which
should be treated as such if the story be true. May I
point out, thero being an appetite for this sort of thing,.
that largo sums of money are raised from silly people on
the faith, the truth, of these secret stories, hence your
responsibility. I intend, when Parliament meets in
October, to bring theso proselytising frauds before the
House, unless in the meantime I can get more light on.
tho subject."” £

. Perhaps, ncedless to say, Mr. Young got no reply to
this from the Rev. author. It is safo %oalibol a rclli;‘g]iou
n general terms; if the Rev. Townsend gave real names
and addresses ho would render himself amenable to the
law for his statements, and that would not suit the
purveyors of this class of “ literature.’’

In a vecent number of the Lraprr wo referred to
K_xlkga with regard to the -uso of foreign soap. We
mentioned that Kilkee, as most people were aware, was
in Iveland. However, on visi ing a “ Grand’’ concert
on the 23rd inst., held in a hall, the property of a
zetired colonel of the British “ Awmy,”” ono would think
Kilkeo was roally not in Ircland at'all. Tor some days
beforfel\a-ud large posters announcing the concert woro
plex}hfully displayed on the walls, and the foot of the
notice bore the words—*Proceeds for local purposes.’’
Now this footnote aroused tho suspicions of our cor-
respondent, especially as he had heard it whispered that
ths concart was to be “ denominational,” or, in other
words, the clever wording, “Proceeds for local pur-
posps,” was, no doubt, to be used as a ““ blind ’’ in order
to induce “all creeds and classes *’ to give their moncy
to an entertainment, the proceeds of which would be
handed over to the funds of local “ Saved  institutions.
The concert was of the usual “ God-Save-the-King
type. The “respectable’’ audience were treated to a
cake walk by one Julian Hickey, son of the local doctor
who sends his sons to a London Medical School.  We
wonder could Hickey, who, by the way, is an ex-Clon-
gownian, dance an Irish reel? Oh, no! Nothing so
common! Soveral items of the type usually met with
at th: concerts were gone through—songs of every
nationality under the sun—but, of course, no Gaelic.
The audience returned to their homes sadder, but prob-
ably no wiser, at a late hour to the strains of “ Damn
the Idolators.”

In appointing Mr. J. J. O'Kelly (Sceilg) as tra-
veller, the firm of M. H. Gill and Son show that they
are feeling the country’s pulse. Mr. O’Kelly is one of
the most racy and vigorous products of the Ivish Ire-
land movement. He is amongst the most idiomatic
writers of Irish that exist at the present time. Did
the Irish movement not exist, he would probably be
now a prominent figure in Buffalo or New Jersey. If
our country is to prosper, we must keep at home not
only farm labourers and bricklayers, but also men of
literary talent and business capacity.

The Belfast News Letter is still screeching like a Bil-
lingsgate fish-woman. An article in the Daily Neus,
a London paper, has got oun the Belfast News Letter's
nzrvez, and it has gone into hysterics. The distraught
News Letler screeches:—“The Irish Protestants are de-
scribed as an Anglicised class. A class that for centuries
has proved its fidelity to England, often at:great per-
sonal sacrifices, is now taunted with being Anglicised
by this English newspaper.”” When people commence
to screech they often throw reason to the winds, so our
screeching orange contemporary did not see how ridicu-
lous it was in complaining of a class that “ for centuries
has proved its fidelity to England *’ being taunted with
being Anglicised !

The London Daily News said that ‘ To-day we are
at length realising that a contented, successful
Ireland must mean a government of Ireland
in accordance with Irish ideas.”  The ‘irate News-
Letter puts her arms akimho and shouts:— What aro
Trish ideag? There are Nationalist ideas and Unionist
ideas, Roman Catholic ideas and Protestant ideas; but
to write of ‘ Irish ideas’ is intentionally misleading.”

The New-Letter becomes delightfully and uncon-
sciously comic when it states further down in its article
—“There is no Party of ascendancy in Ireland, but
that of the Nationalists and the priests.””

Qur Orange contemporary qugtes the following from

the Daily News:—*‘ The tiny Ulster faction, which re-
‘prosents but a remote fragment of that province, repre-

sonts ‘also a power which is dying or dead.” ;Thls
naturally added fuel to the ire of the Billingsgate News
of Bolfast, and we are told:—‘ The Ulster members
who are thus spoken of, with the two members for
Dublin University, represent more than one-fourth of
the population, and certainly more than half the intelli-
gence, wealth, and industry of the country.” We
think ‘we have hoard some of that tale before—*‘ Half
the intelligence, wealth and industry ”’!  And yet some
of the Trinity College crowd can’t spell, the Rathmines
rates are 10s. 6d. in the £, and the Great Northcrn
Railway Company is afraid to throw its clerkships open
to genuine competitive examination !

The Billingsgate Letter of Belfast winds up this way :
—“The Nationalists and their allies have no love for
Belfast. It is a standing argument against Home Rule,
and a rebuke to all those who represent the poverty Aof
Iroland as due to bad laws made by the Imperial Parlia-
ment. Belfast and Ulster have prospered under these
laws. They received no favours. Why have the other
provinces not prospered? Perhaps it is because they
are dominated by ideas which the Daily News describes
as ‘mere midsummer madness.”  Yeb its policy is to
place the men who have made Ulster prosperous under
the control of those who have made the other provinces
miserable ; to allow idle agitators to dip their hands into
the pockets of the industrious and thrifty ; and to place
Protestantism, and all that it stands for, at the mercy
of the most ignorant and bigoted priesthood in Europe.”

It will be observed that ‘‘the surpliced ruffians of
Maynooth *’ get the last sting. But on a question of
fact, is Ulster so prosperous as ranters like the Billings-
gate Letter are constantly asserting? On looking mp
Thom’s Directory we find that Ulster, with a popula-
tion of 1,582,826 in 1901, was valued in 1905 at
£5,216,072, whilst Leinster, with only a population of
1,152,829 in 1901, was valued in 1905 at £5,054,134.
We work these figures out at Ulster’s valuation some-
thing under £3 6s. 0d. per head of the population,
whilst Leinster works out at something over £4 7s. 0d.
per head of the population. In face of these figures,
what are the value of the screeches of the Billingsgate
Letter about the prosperity of Ulster ?

The “ truly National > Freeman, and, needless to say,
the ha’penny o gan of the International Imposition, are
puffing the Fleet, that is to provide a sort of side attrac-
tion or peep-show at Kingstown during the Horse Show
Week.  About half a page of the Zndependent, of Tucs-
day, is taken up with illustrations of British Jack and
his ships; even a cat, that it appears is a pet on one of
the ships, has had its photograph taken, and a repro-
duction of it duly appears in the so-called Nationalist
Independent! Of course the tramways expect to make
a bit out of the traffic that the new Peep-Show at
Kingstown will create, and Bung of Kingstown has
no doubt placed extra orders with James Talbot Power,
the grog manufacturer, who, we notice, is on the com-
mittee that has to do with feteing the Atlantic Fleet.

Probably the Naval authorities calculate that the
Peep-Show will prove a fruitful recruiting stroke of
business; for Irish youths from all parts of the country
will be in town during Horse Show Week. Amidst all
the press puffery and arrangements for entertaining the
Jack Tars we hear no protest against the awful treat-
ment to which unfortunate Catholic men, who join the
Atlantic Fleet, are subjected. Perhaps James
Talbot Power, the Freeman, the Independent, and all
the rest might think it ‘‘ discourteous,”” or mayhap,
“ dragging religion into hospitality,”” to say boo con-
cerning the treatment accorded to Catholics who join

. this Fleet now on show at Kingstown. The Atlantic
Fleet has, we are informed on excellent authorily,
just- ome, ome solitary “ Idolatrous’ chaplain;
needless to say, there is a “saved ’’ minister on every
ship of the Atlantic Fleet. When the solitary ““ Idola-
trous’’ priest is on shore, at the Hospital, at Gibraltar,
there is then no priest whatever with the Fleet, and
when he is with the Fleet there js no priest at the
Hospital.  So that in connection with this Fleet which
James Talbot Power, the truly Catholic Freeman, and
others are booming, many a poor Irish lad, or man has no
doubt to die without the consolation of his religion, and
is buried like a dog.  This is inevitably what joining the
Atlantic Fleet means for James Talbot Power’s co-
religionists.  There are almost 2,000 Catholics with the
Atlantic Fleet, and these are mostly Irish. ‘What
sort of consciences have Catholics who boom a thing
like this Fleet, where their co-religionists are denied
almost absolutely the services of the miunisters of their
religion. We emphasise the Catholic side of the matter
only on this occasion, as the kopje-climbing Independent
and Frééman, and tame Catholics like James Talbot
Power, ought at least to be concerned with the souls of
their co-religionists, if the purely Irish aspect of the
matter makes no appeal to them. What have tho
officers or men of this Fleet ever done for Ireland, except
draw a large proportion of their pay out of the taxes
which help to keep this country poor and stagnant?
- Apart from any other consideration, the lack of provi-
sion for the religious needs of Catholics in the Fleet, now
acting as recruiting agent and Peep-Show at Kingstown,

Trisz CEMENT WANTED.

Waterford, August 27th, 1906.
Dear Sir,—In your issue of last week a correspondent
from Ardee, asked was there any cement made in Ire-
land. T am glad to inform him there is a flourishing
cement works at Drinagh, Co. Wexford, where every
ounce of their cement is manufactured.—Yours truly,

Parrick J. MURRAY.

AGRICULTURAL MACHINERY AND C0-OPERATiVE STORES.

Dublin, 25th August, 1906.

Dear Sir,—The letter from the Manager of the Lom-
bardstown Co-operative Agricultural Stores, appearing
in your columns this week, would be very amusing if 1t
did not display an extraordinary ignorance—real or
pretended—of business methods.

The Wexford Engineering Co. have a perfect right
to conduct their business in whatever way seems best to
themselves, and the appointment of Agents is a recog-
nised custom in their trade. ~ Very honourably, as any
impartial person will admit, when they received the
enquiry from the Lombardstown Agricultural Stores,
they referred that Company to their District Agent.
But the “ self-respect of the L. A.S. (God bless the mark!)
would not allow them to deal with a “middleman’
(what are ¢hey?) one-half their business size. A more
ridiculous objection has never been invented. If busi-
ness concerns declined to deal with makers or agents
for no other reason but that they were not half their
own size, with whom would Guinness’s deal?

There is no parallel, as any business man knows, be-
tween buying drapery, flour, boots, etc., and buying
cycles and agricultural machinery. The latter are pro-
prietary articles, and the universal custom is to grant.
agencies.

The manager should have gone a little further and
let us know how he suited his customer. Having failed
to deal with the Wexford Engineering Co. did he invest

THE_UNIVERSITY QUESTION : AN OYERLOOKED
POINT.

HERE is nothing which provokes such discussion
as a definite proposal. Home Rule was often
discussed in the House of Commons before Mr. Glad-
stone brought in his famous Bill in 1886. But the dis-
cussions before 1886 were always academic; in 1886
they were something very different, indeed. So with
tho everlasting University Question. It was a dull sub-
ject until the recent  Trinity offer *’ was made; now it
has become a live subject. Like others, I naturally
want to say something about it. I should not do so,
however, did I not feel that I was about to draw atten-
tion to a point or two which seem to have escaped the
notice of the rest of the writers on the subject.

The three possible settlements which the Bishops are
understood to be willing to consider are—(1) a Catholic
University ; (2) a Second College in Dublin University,
and (3) a New College under the Royal University. The
first of these would, no doubt, be the ideal settlement
for us Catholics, but then, circumstances—and the Non-
conformist conscience—being as they are, I fear this par-
ticular settlement is so very “ideal’’ that there is no
us2 in discussing it.  About the scheme of a second
College in Dublin University I should, however, like to
say a few words. Several of those who have written on
the whole question appear unwilling to discuss the
details of the “ Seécond College *’ scheme, and even to
resent any invitation to do so. I think this unfortu-
nate. In Ireland we are apt sometimes to favour things
which strike our imagination without waiting to consider
how they would work out in practice.

The supposed recommendation of the “Second Col-
lege ”’ scheme is that it would enable Catholics to share
in the prestige of Dublin University. The cherishing
of any such supposition seems to me to show how little
real thought was ever given to the working out of this
particular scheme. Suppose a Second College were set
up in Dublin University—or rather, under Dublin
University—what would happen? Simply  this.
Catholics alone would attend the new College, while Pro-
testants, and possibly certain Catholics, too, would re-
sort to Trinity. Now, the graduates of the new College
would describe themselves as “ B.A. (Dub.),”” or “M.A.
(Dub.),”” as the case might be, but the graduates of
Trinity would describe themselves in the old familiar
way, as “B.A., T.C.D.,” or “M.A,, T.C.D.” 1In that
case—and we know very well that that is what would
happen—what would become of our “share in the pres-
tige”’ of Dublin University? And, mind, you could
not prevent the Trinity men so differentiating themselves.
The past Trinity men have almost invariably described
themsolves so; how could you forbid or prevent the per-
petuation of the historic practice? “Dublin Univer-
sity ”’ is really only a technicality ; the ¢ University *’
only oxists whonever the Board meets, and is put by on
the shelf until it meets again. But Trinity College is
a palpable, tangible, visible thing—so very visible that
you cannot be long in Dublin without becoming familiar
with its imposing front. Trinity College has the pres-
tige, such as it may be, and won by mpnopoly, as it may
be; Dublin University is an abstraction, a sort o_f aca-~
demic' fiction. You cannot get hold of the prestige of
Dublin University unless you get hold of Trinity Col-
lege, because Dublin University has no prestige, apart
from that of Trinity College. The net result, then, of
the “Second College’’ scheme, so far as the famous
o presbig‘eﬁ* goes, would be that the Catholics in the
new -Collége would be isolated un(_ie_r the denomination
of “M.A, (Dub.),”” while the Trinity men, under the

;

* old historic denomination of “M.A., T.C.D.,” would

have the prestige all for themselves, just as before!

Bub that is not the only drawback of the  Second
College * scheme. The idea is, I understand, that ’bhls
new College should be “in every respect oqual’’ to
Trinity College. How could that possibly bo? Trinity
would retain_its ancient site in tho heart of Dublin.
Where would the new College be? At Inchicore, or

- Drumcondra, or Terenure, or at Donnybrook{ It would
have to be somewhere in the suburbs.  How, then,
could its resident studemts avail themselves of Trinity
library? Or of the National Library? Yes, but the
new College would have a great library of its own! I
do not know about that. Great libraries do not grow
so rapidly as mushrooms. As to size, it would be a
long time until the new College had a library as large
as Trinity or the National ; as to quality, it could never
have some of the things that Trinity has—manuscripts
of unique value and interest, Irish, Hebrew, Greck, and
Latin. The buildings of the new College, if they are
9 be ““ in every respect equal ”’ to Trinity College, would
have, of course, to be equal in size; they could there-
fore not be erected in the city, since there would not
be room for them. Again, to be “ in every respect equal’’
to Trinity, the new College would want to have an cqual
prestige. That it could not have; we cannot, by Act
of Parliament, set up a new College with a prestige
ready made—with Burke, Goldsmith, Swift and Lecky
already educated there. In fine, it would not be pos-
sible for the new College to open its doors on an equality
with Trinity in anything, save merely in size. It would
not be equal in alumni, in site, library, prestige, or
(what is as important as any) in having a staff bred by
itself. How anyone ever imagined that a new College
“In every respect equal’’ to Trinity could ever be set
up, I cannot understand. We might set up a College,
had we proper funds, which would in good time outrival
Trinity; but to set up a College, equal ab initio to
Trinity, is a thing perfectly impossible. I can under-
stand, in view of these considerations, a large deal of
the reluctance to discuss the details of the Second Col-
lege scheme. In view of the drawbacks I have indicated,
I think we may regard the Second College scheme as
quite out of the question. It would matter nothing
how the new College was governed, or staffed, so long
as some of the defects I have adverted to remained,
and, above all, so long as its graduates were de facto
left. without any share of the prestige of Trinity.  All
of which being so, I hope we shall have heard the last
of, the “ Second College ”’ scheme as a solution of the
University question.

About the other solution of this question, that of a
new College under the Royal University, I hope to say
something next weok, especially about that kind of Col-
lege recommended by the Robertson Commission. Mean-
while my readers can reflect a good deal about the obvious
way in which the Second College men would remain
destitute of all the prestige of the degrees given in the
name of Dublin University. IMAAL.

—these may be had in many gardens if not in.all. Just

through whom the name survives, or the aged mother,
who outlived her husband, all the sons, and all the

least, while those golden heads are happy under the
trees, it is the children who come to the mind, bringing
with their fancied selves thoughts of their times and
the thoughts of those times. The Sheares children were
born into a rarely cultured home. All that tended to
a quiet conservatism surrounded it—property, wealth,
acknowledged position, studious habits, many friends,
many books. As the old people say, what a “ scatter
came upon it all! Sitting here thinking of the thing,
or at least of the heart of it, one begins to understand
how great must have been the influence of that terrible
upheaval of the traditional in France. ~O’Connell used
to tell how John and Henry Sheares witnessed the
execution of Louis XVI.: they bribed some soldiers of
the National Guard to exchange uniforms with them;
thus garbed, they stood close to the guillotine and
“had a splendid view.” ‘“How could you have done
it?’ was asked. ““From love of the cause,”’ was the
reply. How came the love of that “cause’ into the
birdlings of these quiet, cultured parents, of this quiet
nest? The boys must have been adventurous, it is true,
four or five of them meeting with untimely ends, one of
them being drowned while saving John, who was bath-
ing. Yet, on the other hand, the father was a man of
property and a banker, a profession to which liberalism
ought to be suspect. ~And tradition would seem to be
simply going its road when one brother chooses the navy
as a profession, another the army, another the law.
Madden, in his memoir of the brothers, tells how he,
himself, picking up a volume at a bookstall, the ¢ Beau-
ties of the Spectator’’ by name, discovered by an in-
scription on it the fact that it was a prize awarded to
John Sheares for his excellence at Virgil—a pathetic
enough find.  From such hints as these we get the idea
of a home breathing quiet, as quiet as the old houss
looks now, seen through, the laden trees. Then, what
a heat must the up-flaming of the Revolution have put
in the blood of those who hearkened to its ideals, touched
it in its home, heartened it, mayhap with words such as
Wordsworth gave it, not foreseeing the gentlo years
that were to take him into very different times. Yet,
here it is not easy to understand, this place is so quiet,
was so quiet then.  In books it is easy to see effects
springing from causes. Here one begins to reach the
causes by means of the effects—but so slowly! These
vory trees bore fruit when John and Henry were boys;

tho stream has not changed a note of its song; autumn *
evenings were as benignant then as now ; this garden had .

then the same heart of peace—an opiate against the
violent. ~ Bub John and Henry Sheares are violently
dead! And it would seem as if one could never amass
enough of causes, sitting here in peace, to account for
tho great change.

If one lived in their times would one know the reason
of the thing much better?! It may bo doubted. Some
who.livcd then did know, some did not. I have been
looking at a copy of a paper published in 1792 in Cork—
the Evening Post. Tt contains a full-page account of a

sitbing of the States Goneral, whereat it was dobated
whether the man, Louis Capet, should be brought to

a_ltexj. one’s impression. The news from France is curious

Not many knew. The paper would lead one to suspect

who lived through & time of national stress, who knew

served up in history’s concise pages. e may keep ask-
ing himself “Is all history like this”? But surely it is
also a pathetic thing to know that the historian may
see our times more truly than we who are its offspring!
What ideas had the peasants of Wexford about the
French Revolution?  Yet the French Revolution did
much in leading them on.

Many who witnessed the execution of King Louis
might have had some dim foreseeing that their own fate
was being weighed ; but did these two poor Corkmen ever

selves close to a gibbet in Dublin. History, I think,

cleared the sky, autumn’s frost-jewelled hand has
blanched it; ’tis chilly, earth and sky!—it was the
children who were in our mind—why now the vaults of
St. Michan’s? Thus goes thought ever. Let us to the
warm-lipped gossiping. LEE.

Soon after the Gaelic Revival had spread,

And trade with the language begun to make head,

"Twas the custom to think that the men for our soil

‘Were merchants, producers and creatures who toil;

And none were so fitted the nation to build

As coarse-minded, masculine workers well skilled.

Ah, those -were hard times for our gossoons refined,

Whoss souls shrank appalled from rude work of that
kind,

And never could bear their high birth to disgrace

By mixing in commerce material and base.

Och, sure it was shocking what light then was thrown

Upon our sweet dandies of culture and tone,

‘Because the young bucks only wanted to be

Grand barristers, doctors and artists, you sce,

Not mere vulgar traders, and experts who toil

In beastly machinery filthy with oil.

In those depraved times any day you might meet

Such swest, young gossoons hanging round Grafton
Street,

And the sweetest and purtiest boy of all these

‘Was Seumas O’Brien, a young bard, if you please.

Ah, Seumas, ’twas you had the culture and art,

The soul that despised the base mercantile mart.

You never intended your culture to stain

By handiwork dear to mere Philistine brain.

And, faix, sure enough the sweet boy was too grand

TFor any vile work that is done with the hand.

But oh for misfortune, and oh for his fate,

Such work he was put to, it grieves me to state.

Ah, Seumas, who knowing your soul’s mighty bent

Could dream to a gas-house ono day you’d be sent—

Tmagine a gas-house—great Scott—for a mind

The star-spangled universe hardly confined. :

Yet, such was the case; for his coarse-minded sire

Lonked down with contempt on all sons of thel lyre,

- And held that no doctor, or axﬁst or seer
“Was quarter as good as a gas engineer.

With such vulgar notions inspired he—alas!—
Apprenticed his fiery-souled son to the gas.

Ah, Soumas, look back at the beautiful moon,
Tor tho door of the gas-house will close on you soon;
Not much longer now have you leisure to dream
Upon her pale glory and mystical beam.

Farewell to the Johnnies, from them you must pass,
Your pedigree soon will be tarnished with gas;
Tarowell to the posers whom butterfly ties

And circular cadies so well advertise;

Trarewell all who’d scorn such labour to do;
Farewell to tho National Library, too.

Aggricved were the posers when tidings came on
That Seumas O’Brien to a gas-house had gone.
Their grief had, however, a very brief span,

For Seumas away from the gas-house soon ran.
The moment the bard the foul business beheld
His instinet pootic rebelliously swelled,

And—“ Never,” ho cried, “ will my star-reaching soul
Be chained in this vile, recking, Tartarus hole.
Before a begrimed enginoer I would be

A ycoman Imperial I’d don the khaki.”

With these words heroic he fled out amain,

And never was seen near the gas-house again.

iN yowr issue of the 26th inst. you comment on the
proceeding atb the recent meeting of the ‘ Irish’’

Association of Gas Managers. And you remark, “ We

wonder how many gas managers in Ireland are Irish.”

I regret to inform you that there are only about 15
or 20 gas managers that can be called Irish in this
country.  You also take the following quotation from
the President’s address: —“In Ireland the social grades
differ very much from those across the water; the class
from which we draw apprentices here can ill afford to
keep a lad for several years receiving only a nominal
salary while learning his trade.”

You then state, “ Well, as broad-minded, thoughtful
persons, we sorrowfully admit that Bruce Anderson hit
the country fairly on a sore point there. The class of
people who should be apprentices to such honourable
and useful industries as the gas industry are brought
up ‘ Sweet Boy Graduates,” etc., etc.”  Now, I have
what Mr. Anderson calls the good fortune to be an Irish-
man, and I regret to say, with all due respect to your
good self, the misfortune to be connected with the gas
industry.

I served an apprenticeship of five years without re-
ceiving even a nominal salary under an imported gas
engineer and manager, while learning my trade as a
gas manager. I had a taste for the work, and I did my
best to learn all I could, and within that time I gained
a first-class certificate in gas manufacture from the City
and Guilds, London.

Naturally I thought when I finished my apprentice-
ship I would be sure of a decent appointment at a fair
salary.  After applying for numerous positions I, at
last, with a great deal of trouble, secured one at the
magnificent salary of £1 per week. I think even a
“ sturdy beggar ' would be able to earn more than that,
and he certainly would not have to serve as long an
apprenticeship without receiving any salary. I have
since applied for almost all the vacant gas managerships
within recent years in Ireland, but it was no use, as the
Directors of Gas Companies and Gas Committees prefer
an Englishman or a Scotchman to an Irishman. As one
of the first questions you will bo asked when you are
looking for a position, haye you had any experience in
an English or Scotch Gas Works, or in other words, are
you an Englishman or a Scotchman?

Sometime ago you commented on the Newry Town
Council appointing an Englishman as manager of their

said that they only received applications from managers
of small works in Iveland, and that these applicants
had not any knowledge of the manufacture of: Sulphate

of Ammonia.

It was pointed out to you by a corvespondent of yours
at that time that this statement was not true. And to
show you how ridiculous this objection is to the appoint-
ing of any Irishman to that position I take the fo%lowing
extract from “Newbegging’s Handbook for Gas Engincers
and  Managers” :—“The apparatus required in
its manufacture (Sulphate of Ammonia) is neither
complicated nor costly, the process is free from danger,
and an intelligent labourer can learn it in a week’s
time.”

Even if we admit for the sake of argument that the

Irish Gas Managers did not know how to manufacture

man of the Gas Committee to say that was the reason

works in Ireland, and how ably for the most part they

o supply the gas companies in England or elsewhere with

THE CATHOLIC UNIVERSITY OF MALTA.

T needs great nerve to say anything new about the

University question, but as the case of the Univer-

sity of Malta has not been quoted, I venture to mention
it as an important precedent.

I have before me the “Statute of the University of
Malta,”” kindly sent to me by the Vice-Rector, Dr. J.
J. Reynolds. 1 do not kuow him, but the name suggests
an Hibernian origin. The “Statute’’ is issued from

the Government Printing Office, Malta, and bears the

familiar Lion and Unicorn, but on opening the book the
first line that strikes one’s eyes is: —Aurticle 1.—“ The
Roman Catholic religion is the views of the instruction
to be imparted in every public educational establishment.
No teaching irreconcilable with Catholic principles is
permitted in public Schools.”

Now what is the use of speaking about the
University of Bonn. Here we have the British
Government founding and maintaining a Catholic
University of a more exclusively Catholic character than
ever the Irish Bishops dreamed of. We are told now-
a-day that the Non-conformist conscience will not tolerate
any endowment of Catholic Theology. But there is a
fully-fledged faculty of Theology at Malta. I give the
regulations verbatim : —

The course of Theology extends over four years, and
proceeds in the following order : —

FIRST YEAR.

1.—Dogmatic Theology.—De  Revelatione.—De
Locis Theologicis (daily lectures).

2.—Moral Theology.—De Natura et Moralitate
Actuum Humanorum.—De Conscientia.—De
Legibus.—De Censuris (daily lectures).

SECOND YEAR.
1.—Dogmatic Theology.—De Deo Uno.—De Deo

Trino.—De Deo Creatore (daily lectures).
2.—Moral Theology.—De Pecatis, in genere et in

specie.—De Virtutibus Theologicis. De Vir- .

tute Religionis (daily lectures).
THIRD YEAR.

1.—Dogmatic Theology.—De Incarnatione.—De
Gratia.—De altera vita hominis (daily lectures).

2.—Moral Theology.—De Justitia et Jure.—De Con-
tractibus, in genere et in specie.—De Restitu-
tione (daily lectures). :

FOURTH YEAR.

1.—Dogmatic Theology.—De Sacramentis, in genere
et in specie (daily lectures).

2.—Moral Theology.—De modo administrand Sacra-
menta, in genere et in specie (daily lectures).

As admissions to the Academical Cowrse take place:
overy threo years, the Professors of Thology shall, for
the convenionce of students admitted at the end of the
third year, explain at the beginning of the fourth year
what is indispensably required by such students to under-
stand the subjects to be taught in the fourth year.

Not only is there a Faculty of Theology, but theo-
logical students are evidently favoured, for we read at
Statute No. 245 : — 3

“The degree of Doctor shall be conferred in each of the
TFaculties of Theology—Law and Medicine—on those

students who shall have passed all the annual examina-

tions, on payment of the following registration fees,

viz. : —£2 105, for the Faculty of Theology, and £5 for

the Faculty of Law and Medicine.”

SEPTEMBER 1, 1906.

Here we have the British Government granting to

‘the Maltese what the Irish have craved for during
a century. Not only a Catholic University, —but a Uni-

versity where every student must attend Catholic. instruc-

~ tions, for Ordinance No. 76 states:— A

- “No Catholic student can absent himself from the
course of religious instruction without a special per-
mission from the Rector, and the consent of his father or
guardian.’’

Still more strange, - the University founded by the
British Government with public money is prepared to
recognize the teaching of any Catholic ecclesiastical semi-
nary in the world, for we read in Article 249 : —

“Persons who shall have completed their studies in
a recognised Catholic Institution abroad may be ad-
mitted to these four examinations for the degree of
D.D. ou their showing that they have studied the sub-
Jects specified—and those admitted shall pay £6 as regis-
tration fee before the degree is conferred.”’”  What an
uproar would have arisen if, when Maynooth College
instituted its degrees in Theology a few years ago, it had
demanded an endowment from the Government, and a
special Charter from the Statute.

Yet it appears that the most mediocre priest who ever
passed through Maynooth will be recommended as eli-
gible for his examination as D.D. by the British Gov-
ernment, if he will only take a trip to Malta.

" Obviously we have only to demand a University on the
Malta model to get more than we ever hoped for in our
fondest dreams and yet, not more than the British Gov-
= ernment has already conceded to mere Colonists, for these
% ordinances are signed so recently as May 30th, 1898, by
Arthur Lyon Freemantle, Governor of Malta.

Every year we are training labourers for export, and

cases cost is not the most alarming feature, but scarcity.
There arc times a farmer would go any price, but, as
the saying is, ‘“ Men can’t be got for love or money.””
At the present moment thousands of tons of hay are
rotting in the richest counties because the supply of
labour at hand is utterly inadequate in this trying
scason. Any casual student of rural economics can
see how this problem works out.. If something is not
done, and done speedily, a tillage farm over forty acres
will be an uneconomic holding in a quarter of a century.
The owner of such a holding will not be able to labour
it himself, nor yet to pay labour. Already the in-
creased cost and diminished output of hired labour has
swamped the reduction in rent on such holdings. More
men of this class have gone bankrupt in the last twenty
years than of any other section of the farming com-
munity. If this dearth of labour continues, all hope of
Ireland as a tillage country is lost. The good lands will
be let out in grass and the bad lands will revert to
heath and rushes. The country will be given over
wholesale, body and bones, to the grazier. The tillage
farmer depends on the labourer, and the towns on both,
and with them the Irish nation will disappear. Verily,
the Labour Question is the great problem which con-
fronts Ireland in the New Century—a problem to test
her statesmanship and to task her vitality; a problem
she must solve in the next twenfy years or—die! If I
might compare Landlordism to a cancer devouring the
substance of the people, the present Labour crisis might
be compared to a general paralysis, whose deadly stroke
has rendered powerless the energies and resources of the
nation. Its unnerving, paralysing influence may be
seen in the collapse of our manufactures, commerce and
industries.  The progress of this fell disease may be
watched from day to day in any department of Agricul-
ture.

‘“Imaal’’ talks of the Seven Acts, of the disappear-
ance (?) of the Mud Cabin, allows the Acts may have
done more good than harm, and goes on with a jaunty
air as if the problem were solved, or in a fair way to be
solved ; whereas, anyone acquainted with the realities of
rural life must know that the Seven Acts have touched
no more than the fringe of the Labour Question.

I am sorry I cannot at the moment lay my hands on
statistics to reinforce the facts familiar to every farmer.
But if T were to give the number of houses erected or in
course of erection under the present scheme as 20,000,
I would not be doing injustice to the zeal of District
Councils or of Parliament. 20,000 labourers to
20,000,000 acres gives one labourer to 1,000 acres.
Thus it will be seen that the effect of all past schemes
is infinitesimal in finding houses for the labourers or
labour for the farmers. To give the Irish farmer any
chance against France, Belgium, Denmark, etc., there
must be a labourer on every forty acres, allowing for
small holdings and domestic servants.

How do we try to supply this need at present?
1. By domestic or living-in servants; 2. By cottiers;
3. By out-labourers or day labourers. The first two
classes are diminishing in quantity and quality every
year. But it is in the last class that the great falling
off is. The explanation is simple. The first class is
incapable of maintaining itself and must be supported
from without. The cottier class—the survivors of the
old living-out labourers—is now too small to count.
When the supply of domestic servants for large farms
has to be drawn from small holdings the labour of these
farms is thrown on an old man and children too young
to hire; so that even small holdings are going out of
cultivation and becoming uneconomic. While the out-
labourer—the only class who could maintain a cheap,
constant and efficient labour supply—are swiftly and
surely disappearing. The out-labourer, under normal
conditions, is a man who, having served his apprentice-
ship to agriculture as a living-in servant, saves some
money, settles in a house of his own, and proceeds to
rear a family for the upkeep of the Labour Market.
But such normal conditions, of course, do not apply to
Ireland. IHere, when a young labourer reaches that
stage when in a normal country he would think of

‘marriage, there is no possibility of his setting up house.
There is no supply of decent, sanitary houses. There
s no supply of houses of any sort, oven the mud walls
his forefathers were familiar with, nor any possibility
of erecting them under the existing Land Tenure. ' If
he has any money by him there is nothing he can invest
b in unless a passage to America. Whether this will
improve his lot remains to be tried, but omne ignotum
pro magnifico, and of this, at least, he is sure that he
cannot improve himself at home. The present schemes
do not provide accommodation for the tenth part of the
Agricultural labourers. Even Mr. Bryce’s scheme of
30,000 houses more would not afford onc-quarter of the
houses necessary. The Council’s cottages are often in-
ferior to the landlord’s cottages in England. But the
Council’s cottages are far superior to the hovels and
rookeries in which nine-tenths of our labourers live.
Many of their houses verge on the class which the
nedical officer would condemn as ‘‘ unfit for human
habitation.”” ‘ Imaal ’ says that ‘‘ the last of the mud
cabins must by this time have vanished.”

What part of Ireland does he hail from? There are
scores of mud cabins within a cycle ride of Dublin.
They have them in the North, they have them in the
South, in the Centre. I would like to know where they
have them not, unless the nature of the soil does not
supply the material.

Nor are mud cabins the worst houses labourers have
to put up with.  Worse far are the slated, stone houses
in villages and hamlets where the labourers hunted
from the lands have found shelter. These dwellings
add to the horrors experienced by Jurgis in Chicago,
the odour of antiquity, for they repose upon centuries
of dirt and disease. In filthy, leaking, rotten tene-
ments, without backyards or any sanitary accommoda-
tion whatever, a family in every room, separated from
their next neighbour by lath and plaster walls and un-
sealed floors, our labourers are piled on top of one
another, in human rookeries. A mud cabin on the
mountain side, with fresh air, pure water and pure soil
and the privacy of family life, were far preferable.
Such are the houses of the labouring classes.

Yet we wonder why the flower of our manhood and
womanhood is emigrating, why the labour supply in
every department of industry is becoming more and
more insufficient and inefficient. We shout: ‘‘Stop
Emigration,” ‘‘ Increase Tillage,”” ‘‘ Encourage IHome
Manufactures,”” but all in vain. The greatest IHome
manufacture, men and women, is dying out, the supply
of Labour is being cut off, and the springs of national
prosperity dried up at the source.

““ Imaal,”” with truth and justice, ascribes the present
condition of the Labour Market to the Old Land
System. But he scarcely realises the full effects of that
iniquitous institution—Landlordism. It will be the
duty of the future historian to demonstrate how land-
lordism vitiated every department of public life. None
felt its vicious influence more fully than the Poor Law.
By it the Poor Law Guardians were turned into the
Poor Man’s Persecutors.

In order that the labourer might not become a
burthen on the Rates, ¢.c.,-on the landlords, in his old
age, he was hounded down and exterminated. It was
not merely, as “ Imaal ’’ supposes, that the farmer was
unable to build houses for his labourers. Ile might not
even repair existing ones. The landlord exerted all his
influence by threat and otherwise to get the labourers’
houses pulled down. No tenant at will could resist
such influence. Even a leaseholder, when his lease

Dear Mr. Eprror,—IL have read with pleasure your

‘editorial comment on Dr. Douglas fyde’s reply to the

Dublin Corporation. Dr. Hyde declared that the Gaclic
movement stood for absolute toleration. It opened its
doors to all comers, without distinction of creed or clas?;
“No matber what uniform they wore,”
said Dr. Hyde, “whother black or scarlet or green,

“there was room for them on tho platform of the League.

You, Mr. Editor. give your hearty approval to this
pronouncement, and so, I presume, do all Gaelic
uers. The policy of toleration for all Irishmen,

ted by the Leaguo, certainly appears to be sound.

THE LEADER. 21

Its justification is not far to seek. The Gaelic Leaguo
aims abt putting an Irish  spirit into the
country by reviving its language, studying
its  history, its traditions, its music, dance,
cte. To secure this end it utilises every means
that comes to hand. It realises tho power of numbers
and organisation, and welcomes to its banner all who are
willing to co-operate. If a man be willing to become
a Gaelic Leaguer, his politics do not constitute a disa-
bility. Neithor does his religion, or his profession. It
an Irish Tommy Atkins appeared at a cy4asb meeting
with his O’Growney in his hand, a general stampede
could not be justified on principle. Ifowever one may
despise Tommy’s scarlet jacket when we meet him in the
stroat, as a Gaelic Leaguer we must treat him as a
comrade, Politics are sunk here. We must tolerate all
genuine Leaguers, whether their coats are black, or
scarlet, or green. ’

This policy of the Gaelic League appears to be reason-
akle, and to rest on sound principles. 2
Now, Mr. Editor, here is another case which appears
to me to be parallel, but in which a parallel policy is

not pursued.

Dr. Douglas Hyde is a distinguished patron of the
Gaelic Athletic Association.

Turning over the pages of the official handbook of the
G.A.A. for 1906, we find the following rule. It is found
twice. First under section headed “ Clubs,” rule 8;
and sccondly under section headed “ Athletic Meetings,”
rule 6. It says (I quote rule 6), ““ That police, soldiers,
militiamen, sailors of the Royal Navy, and pensioners
of the Constabulary, Army or Navy, be not allowed to
compete at athletic meetings under the auspices of the
Association.”’

This is a very drastic rule, and I really cannot see
that it is justified on principle. And if it can be justi-
fied, I believe it is not expedient to enforce it.

I hold no brief for policemen or soldiers,.and if T
question the wisdom of this rule, I do so only in order
to have it discussed, without bias or prejudice. And I
confess that I should be glad to see the rule vindicated.

What I should like to know is this: On what principle
are the parties mentioned in the above rule excluded
from athletic sports, football and hurling matches under
the auspices of the G.A.A.? I want a sound principle;
one that will bear the test of impartial examination ;
one that can be applied all round.

It seems to me that if these men are willing to play
Trish, games, and compete at Irish sports after Irish
fashion, they should at least be tolerated.

Thero is nothing in the Constitution of the G.A.A.

Le ‘Why should the G.A.A. not do the same? Is
there any essential difference between the two cases?

he G.A.A. is non-political. At least I take it to bo

Its aim is clearly set down—“To cultivato and pre-

serve Irish pastimes.” Surely the fact that a man wears

scarlet coat or a spiked helmet does not unfit him for

* this work. He can still cultivate and preserve Irish

games. - Why, then, should we exclude him, since our

object does not demand his exclusion, but rather forbids

it We want to promote Irish games; let us, thereforo,

induce all we can to practise them, no matter what bo

their profession or politics.

But whatever may be said in justification of the ex-
clusion of policemon and soldiers, actually in the service,
from G.A.A. sports and games, what excuse is there for
excluding them once they have thrown off the scarlet or
khaki, and settled down as respectable civilians? These
men are setbled in Ireland. They will bring up their
families in Ireland. Should wo not endeavour to make
them real Irish, to assimilate them to the Irish move-
ment?

An ex-policeman or an ex-soldier is not necessarily an
anti-Irishman. Many of them are, but there are many
who are not. Let us take one of the latter class. Suppose
that after retiring from the “ force ”” he joined a cpaot
studied Irish, and became an enthusiastic
“"'ould it not be monstrous if that man, following up
his newly-awakened Irish instincts, prepared for an
TIrish athletic contest, and applied for permission to com-
pete, only to find himself ‘excluded, debarred? And
why debarred? Not because he was anti-Irish, or even
un-Irish, but because he was an ex-policeman; while
any shoneen, who despised everything Irish, but had the
good fortune not to be a policeman, could compete ad

- Libitum.

Mr. Editor,.you declared in your editorial note that
if you met Wade Thompson at a Gaelic Class you would
suppress your feelings for the nonce, and tolerate him,
reserving to yourself the right to treat him differently
outside the class. This is a good all-round policy. I
believe the Council of the G.A.A. should weigh it, and
see if they cannot adopt it. Could they not treat
Tommy Atkins or Constable Hogg with toleration as a
fellow-worker in promoting Irish pastimes, while they

" might despise both in their official capacities—Yours,
ete.,

DeAR. S1r,—Father O'Leary’s remarks on the Muns-
ter Feis are good—calculated to set one thinking.
I have no Irish, and am unable to learn it—not from
“books anyway. My wife is tho same. But we are both
anxious to give our child, a boy of six, a better chance
than we had .ourselves. The question is how best to do
this?
Tt has been suggested to me to send the mother and
child down from Dublin into some Irish-speaking
. district for a wholo season, and I would do this if good
accommodation could be secured at a figure within
ur reach. Connaught, Munster, or Ulster—any Trish
do. Where can information such as I require be
obtained? It is too late to begin this season, but what

Irishman. .

I would like to see next season is a list of boarding
houses, farm houses, or the like, with full cost of resi-
dence, such a list to be obtained on application to
Headquarters or to secs. of local branches. The English -
railways cater for tourists this way, and I've no doubt
that if tho Gaelic League did this, it would induce
many to make a little sacrifice of money and comfort
for tho sake of the children.

Aunother thing I’m not quite sure of. Supposing this -
child of six goes down to some Irish-speaking district
for six months. He must be put to school where he will
learn English and other subjects through the medium
of English, and, at the same time, he is to be sur-
rounded with Irish outside school hours. Would not
this tend to muddle him?

I should like to hear some authority on the subject.—

ErGHT AND SEVEN ARE FIFTEEN. —WHY?

4 Caps,—The Commissioners of National Educa-
tion have been engaged on this knotty problem for some
time, and have now issued to teachers the result of their
deliberations. The Commissioners say that:—(1)
Eight sticks and seven sticks are fifteen sticks, because
eight sticks and two make one bundle, and two sticks
from seven leaves five; (2) eight and seven are the same
as ten and five, because eight and two are ten, and two
from seven leaves five; (3) therefore, eight and seven are
fifteen. These results are to be repeated by the young
pupils four or five times.’’

It is to be regretted that this decision had not been
arrived at and published to the world in time to be
embodied in Mr. O'Malley’s Leabap ap S1pearhn. Itwill -
of course be given due prominence in future editions of -
that little work. “Mapdan.”
